exemplar.title: The evolution of skyscrapers with respect to outer surface area and the cost of construction.

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, conventional structure with introduction, methodology, calculations and conclusion
Coherent logical development of the exploration, allowing the reader to follow each skyscraper case
Use of numbered figures and headings supports navigation
weaknesses: Table of contents poorly formatted and distracts from the narrative
Excessive background material (UN statistics, club history) dilutes mathematical focus
Repetition of integral formula in each section reduces conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs report is well organized with a clear sequence of introduction, methodology, analysis and conclusion, meriting a band-3 mark. Although coherence and structure are good, the poorly formatted table of contents, unnecessary background digressions and repetition of explanations prevented award of the top band.

strengths: Consistent use of standard calculus notation (â«, Ï, dy/dx) for most calculations
Inclusion of tables, scatter plot, regression line and Desmos screenshots to represent data
Logical progression of mathematical arguments in most sections
weaknesses: Occasional omission of units and differentials (e.g., âdBâ instead of dx)
Axes labels and regression equation missing from some figures
Repetition of algebraic blocks and some weak links in justification (e.g., scaling factors)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical communication is mostly clear and logical, with generally consistent notation and multiple representations. The occasional missing units, inconsistent image formatting and some weak explanatory links place the work in a solid band-3 performance.

strengths: Clear personal connection via the ACE-club experience and choice of skyscrapers
Independent selection of calculus methods and Pearson correlation shows genuine initiative
Narrative reflects a personal line of inquiry beyond classroom examples
weaknesses: Use of routine AA SL techniques limits novelty
Modelling choices (straight lines, basic quadratics) follow standard patterns rather than innovative methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach, consistent with band-2 criteria. The work meets expectations for creativity but does not demonstrate the sustained innovation or depth required for the top band.

strengths: Student acknowledges limitations after each calculation and in a final table
Brief discussion of model shortcomings (e.g., tapering effects) shows emerging reflective thought
weaknesses: Reflections remain superficial and lack deep critical analysis
Evaluation of outcomes is limited to general comments on correlation without quantitative assessment
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection shows some meaningful commentary on the modelling process and outcomes, meriting a band-2 mark. However, the depth and critical insight are not substantial enough for the highest band.

strengths: Use of definite integrals for arc-length and surface-of-revolution formula directly addresses the research aim
Application of Pearson correlation and scaling factors demonstrates solid command of AA SL mathematics
Mathematics is largely commensurate with course level and produces coherent, relevant results
weaknesses: Several minor computational and notation errors (unit mis-labels, omitted dx) occur
Some model-fitting choices (e.g., linear model for Trade Centre) lack detailed justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs relevant AA SL mathematics comprehensively and effectively, with a thorough understanding displayed in the integration and statistical analysis. Occasional errors prevent top-band perfection but overall usage justifies a band-5 award.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations accompany most calculations, but blocks of algebra are repeated verbatim and some logical links (why scale factors are applied, justification of linear cost model) are weak.  The argument is understandable but not fully clear or rigorous, fitting band 2.

Several computational and conceptual errors ariseâmis-labelled units (mÂ² vs million square feet), repeated incorrect area totals, and an arc-length element written as âdBâ.  These mistakes indicate only âsome understanding with errorsâ.  The strand therefore fits band 2.

The student supplies tables, a scatter plot with regression line and several Desmos screenshots, so multiple forms are present.  However axes lack scale/units, screenshots are pixelated, and the regression line equation is typed separately rather than shown on the graph.  The usage is therefore âsomeâ but not yet effective.

The student follows a conventional IA sequenceâintroduction, methodology, individual calculations, analysis, conclusion and limitationsâwhich allows the reader to follow the work without major jumps.  Although the Table of Contents is poorly formatted, each later section generally delivers on what the heading promises and the discussion proceeds chronologically from the earliest to the most recent skyscraper.  Frequent digressions (e.g.
 lengthy quotes of UN statistics) prevent the piece from reaching the highest band, but overall coherence is maintained.

While the idea of approximating complex towers with different functions shows some initiative, the mathematical techniques (straight-line segments, basic quadratics, Pearsonâs r) are routine for AA SL.  Little novelty is displayed in the way tools are applied, so only âsome creativity or initiativeâ is evident.

The student analyses residuals only qualitatively (âactual cost is higherâ), and no alternative models or error estimates are considered.  This level of commentary constitutes basic, not thoughtful, analysisâplacing the work in band 1.

Evaluation of outcomes is limited to claiming a âstrong positive correlationâ and suggesting broader data or simulations for future work.  There is no quantitative assessment of model accuracy or implications beyond a single sentence, so the evaluation remains limited.

Reflection is confined to brief comments after each calculation (âthis may not account for taperingâ) and a limitations table at the end.  These statements acknowledge shortcomings but do not probe them in depth, meeting the descriptor for limited reflection.

Standard symbols (\(\int,\,\pi,\,y,\,\frac{dy}{dx}\)) are used, but notation is occasionally incorrectâe.g. âdBâ for arc-length element, inconsistent units, and duplication of the word âSurface area=â.  These inconsistencies keep the strand in the âsome appropriateâ band.

The narrative is framed around the studentâs ACE-club experience and their own choice of five specific skyscrapers, indicating a clear personal approach.  However, depth of customisation is limited by readily available data and common calculus tools, so the work meets but does not exceed band 2.

Most text is relevant to surface-area modelling or cost analysis, yet the write-up is wordy and repeats explanations of the same integral formula for every building.  Irrelevant background on world urbanisation and club membership adds bulk.  The work is therefore âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ, matching band 2.

Definite integrals, arc-length surface-of-revolution formula and correlation statistics are all directly relevant to the aim of relating surface area to cost; each is applied to produce results.  Despite some extraneous background, the mathematics used is clearly pertinentâhence ârelevant mathematics used appropriatelyâ (band 3).

Choosing to derive surface area via calculus and then relating it to cost shows a personal line of inquiry that goes beyond textbook examples.  Independent selection of Pearson correlation and cost-efficiency metric signals âsignificant evidenceâ but the modelling is still largely standard, not outstanding.

Headings, sub-headings and final summary table give the exploration a recognisable structure; each skyscraper is dealt with in a repeatable pattern of limits â integral â scaling.  Figures are numbered and referenced, supporting navigation.  Nevertheless, placement of images sometimes interrupts sentences and the opening TOC is confusing, so the organisation is good rather than exemplary.

Integral calculus and Pearsonâs r lie within AA SL syllabus and are applied at that level.  Although scaling factors and curve-fitting are not fully justified, the bulk of the mathematics is commensurate with course expectations, deserving band 3 for level appropriateness.

annotations.comment: The rationale includes extensive background on urbanisation statistics that digresses from the mathematical focus. Trim or summarise this context to maintain conciseness and relevance to the surface-area modelling.
The aim statement is verbose and repeats terms. Refine it into a single, concise sentence that specifies the variables, methods (definite integrals, correlation), and intended comparison.
Figure 1.0 is used effectively but the axes lack scale markers or units, making interpretation of trends difficult. Label axes clearly with units to strengthen the representation.
The surface-of-revolution formula is given as â«âÏy dB but labels B as âbase areaâ rather than arc-length element. Replace B with s (arc length) and clarify d s = â(1+(dy/dx)Â²)âdx.
The narrative repeats the integral formula before each building calculation, which is redundant. Introduce the formula once and refer back to it to improve conciseness.

The title and introductory statement lack a clearly defined research question, making the focus of the exploration ambiguous. Consider formulating a concise question to guide the investigation and help the reader immediately understand the aim.
The table of contents appears as raw HTML markup and is poorly formatted, hindering navigation. Reformat headings and page numbers in a clean, structured layout to improve readability.
The choice of linear model y=0x+0.79 for the Trade Centre profile is stated but not justified. Explain how the hit-and-trial fitting was conducted and why this model is appropriate.
The reported surface area value (37.724â¦) is not accompanied by units. Always include âunitsÂ²â (e.g., mÂ²) after numeric results to avoid ambiguity.
When summing segment areas (37.724+0.741â¦), the operations should show the integration variable dx and intermediate units to clarify the aggregation process.
The scale factor calculation (38.47Ã541/13.2) is applied without derivation. Provide a brief justification for how the numerator and denominator were determined.
The JHC Centre section provides limits and the equation y=0x+0.75 but lacks explanation of why a constant function fits this shape. Discuss the modelâs assumptions.
The derivative for y=0.001xÂ²-0.02x+0.45 is incorrectly given as (xâ10)/500. The correct derivative is 0.002xâ0.02. Please revise the calculation.
In the SWFC integral expression, the differential element âdxâ is omitted. Include âdxâ in all integral statements for complete mathematical notation.
The piecewise integration steps for Burj Khalifa are shown but the total surface area calculation is buried in text. Summarise the final aggregated result in a clear equation or table.
When summing segment areas for Burj Khalifa, the rounding of intermediate values varies. Adopt consistent significant figures or decimal places throughout.
Figure 2.1 caption is present but the plotted curve lacks axis labels and units. Add clear axis titles and units to improve interpretation.
The raw data table mixes mÂ² units with a heading indicating âmillion square feet.â Verify and correct the unit labels for consistency.
Regression coefficients a and b are reported but there is no mention of confidence intervals or residual analysis. Include brief diagnostics to support the strength of the model.
The expected cost computation for Shanghai Tower references an incorrect fraction â1/2.â Revisit the algebra to ensure the numerical result matches the regression equation.
The cost-per-square-metre calculations present clear equations but omit unit annotations (e.g., USDâ/mÂ²). Include units in both numerator and denominator.
Figure 2.5 scatter plot does not display axes labels, units, or the regression equation on the graph. Enhance this representation for clarity of interpretation.
The conclusion summarises findings but does not critically reflect on the limitations of the modelling assumptions. Include deeper reflection on how these affect the validity of results.
The limitations table lists improvements but the justification column repeats sample-size issues. Refine the justifications to correspond uniquely to each limitation.
References follow mixed formatting with inconsistent use of italics and punctuation. Adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA) and apply it uniformly.
